players.name: Arch Manning
players.bio: The most anticipated quarterback in college football history, Arch Manning carries the weight of a dynasty name while carving out his own legacy in burnt orange. The nephew of Peyton and Eli Manning, and grandson of Archie, Arch became the highest-rated recruit in the On3 Consensus era when he signed with Texas as a five-star in the 2023 class. After backing up Quinn Ewers for two seasons, Manning took sole ownership of the starting job in 2025 and delivered: 3,472 passing yards, 29 touchdowns, and a wire-to-wire Heisman candidacy. His football IQ, pocket composure, and ability to dissect zone coverage at the college level are elite. The next Manning chapter is being written in Austin.

strengths: Elite arm talent across all arm angles — touch, zip, and deep ball
Pre-snap IQ and defensive recognition beyond his age
Natural command of RPO, play-action, and no-huddle tempo
Manning family football DNA — processes at pro speed
weaknesses: Live-game pocket movement still developing under sustained pressure
Red-zone efficiency with turnovers can be cleaned up

draft.notes: Consensus #1 overall prospect for the 2027 NFL Draft cycle. Generational pedigree meets elite measurables and production. If he replicates his 2025 season, he is the lock pick.

players.name: Colin Simmons
players.bio: Colin Simmons is the most disruptive edge rusher in college football. The second-ranked player in the 2024 class, Simmons burst onto the scene at Duncanville — one of the premier high school programs in Texas — before choosing the Longhorns over a massive offer sheet. As a true freshman he flashed elite get-off and bend, and by his sophomore season he was a Bednarik Award finalist and SEC Defensive Player of the Year candidate. Simmons plays with a relentless motor, an explosive first step, and the kind of closing speed that offensive tackles cannot account for. He is already being talked about as a top-five pick in 2027.

strengths: Elite first-step explosion and get-off at the snap
Exceptional bend around the corner for his frame
High-effort motor — plays full speed on every down
Power and finesse rush moves; doesn't rely on one trick
weaknesses: Still adding functional strength for longer-side run fits
Needs to diversify pass-rush counters at the NFL level

draft.notes: Early favorite to be the top non-QB in the 2027 draft. His combination of athleticism, production, and age profile is rare.

players.name: Cam Coleman
players.bio: Cam Coleman arrived at Auburn as one of the most physically gifted wide receivers in the 2023 class. With a 6'3" frame, 4.38 speed, and elite ball-tracking ability, he was a five-star for a reason. After two years in the SEC at Auburn, Coleman entered the transfer portal and chose Texas in a massive recruiting win for Steve Sarkisian. Paired with Arch Manning and Ryan Wingo, Coleman gives the Longhorns an embarrassment of riches at receiver. His ability to win at the catch point and turn short gains into big plays makes him one of the most dangerous wide receivers in the country.

strengths: Rare combination of size (6'3") and speed (4.38) at receiver
Elite ball-tracking and high-point ability in contested situations
Route polish improving rapidly under Sarkisian's staff
Yards after catch ability — hard to bring down in the open field
weaknesses: Consistency of effort as a run blocker
Needs more contested-catch reps in traffic at the intermediate level

draft.notes: Could declare after this season. Freakish physical tools make him a first-round lock if production continues. The Manning connection elevates his value further.

players.name: Malik Muhammad
players.bio: Malik Muhammad was the consensus #1 cornerback in the 2024 recruiting class — a Los Angeles product who bypassed SEC and West Coast offers to join Steve Sarkisian's secondary in Austin. Long-limbed and instinctive, Muhammad plays the game with the confidence of a veteran. He reads route combinations, plays the ball in the air, and doesn't get rattled on islands. As a true freshman he was immediately thrown into SEC competition and held his own. As a sophomore, he became one of the most feared corners in the conference. His trajectory is straight up.

strengths: Elite man coverage instincts — rarely beaten at the top of routes
Ball skills: 4 interceptions as a sophomore is elite production
Length and athleticism to mirror receivers in press
High football IQ; reads QBs effectively from the snap
weaknesses: Finishing on tackles in the run game — needs to be a better open-field tackler
Experience in zone systems still developing

draft.notes: Has the profile to be a top-10 pick if development continues. The #1 corner in the 2024 class is living up to his billing.

players.name: Anthony Hill Jr.
players.bio: A five-star linebacker from the 2024 class out of Ryan High School in Denton, Anthony Hill Jr. is the engine of the Texas defense. Playing in the backfield and at linebacker, Hill has the athleticism of a safety and the power of a traditional inside linebacker. He was one of the most sought-after defensive players in the country, and his ability to drop into coverage, blitz off the edge, and make tackles in pursuit has made him one of the most versatile linebackers in college football. A building-block piece for the Longhorns' defensive identity.

strengths: Elite sideline-to-sideline range for a linebacker — safety-like athleticism
Ability to drop into coverage and match up with tight ends and backs
Productive blitzer — 4.5 sacks as a linebacker is elite
High-effort leader and tone-setter for the defense
weaknesses: Block shedding in tight spaces needs to improve at next level
Still learning to read pulling guards and zone-run schemes

draft.notes: The Micah Parsons comp is earned. If he stays healthy, Hill is a first-round linebacker in 2027. Watch his 2026 season closely.

players.name: Ryan Wingo
players.bio: Ryan Wingo was one of the most productive wide receivers in the SEC during his time at Missouri before transferring to Texas. A 6'0" slot weapon with elite acceleration and route-running, Wingo excels in the quick game and after the catch. He and Cam Coleman give Arch Manning two legitimate go-to options at every level of the field. Wingo's ability to separate at the top of routes and his production in clutch moments made him one of the most coveted receivers in the transfer portal. At Texas, he has continued to thrive as the Longhorns' reliable possession receiver.

strengths: Elite short-to-intermediate route running — crisp, precise breaks
Outstanding YAC ability in the open field
Reliable hands in traffic — strong contested catch percentage
Chemistry with Manning built quickly; trusted in critical downs
weaknesses: Deep ball threat is more limited than Coleman — best under 30 yards
Frame limits some press-man matchups against physical corners

draft.notes: Could leave after this year. A dependable slot receiver with elite production, but needs a strong 2026 campaign to push into late-round-1 territory.

players.name: Raleek Brown
players.bio: Raleek Brown is one of the most explosive backs in college football — a blur in open space with elite contact balance and vision that makes him dangerous as both a runner and receiver out of the backfield. Brown made his name at Oklahoma State before entering the portal and choosing Texas. His quickness out of the backfield and ability to make defenders miss at any level of the field fit perfectly in Steve Sarkisian's spread-friendly run game. Brown is a mismatch nightmare — his pass-catching ability forces defenses to keep a linebacker or safety on him at all times.

strengths: Burst and top-end speed at 5'8" — elite first gear
Exceptional vision and cutback ability behind the line
Pass-catching skills make him a legitimate receiving back
Contact balance — hard to tackle one-on-one in open space
weaknesses: Frame means he can be slowed down in between-the-tackles runs
Pass protection needs work as a pro prospect

draft.notes: A small back who plays big. The receiving ability and explosion make him attractive as a satellite back, but frame concerns keep him outside the first two rounds.

players.name: Tristan Leigh
players.bio: The story of Tristan Leigh's recruitment is one of the most dramatic in recent memory. The #1 offensive tackle in the 2022 class signed with Texas A&M out of Paul VI High School in Fairfax, Virginia — a program powerhouse. After two years in College Station, Leigh entered the transfer portal and chose Texas, delivering one of the most significant portal wins in program history. His combination of length, footwork, and athleticism make him a potential top-10 NFL Draft pick, and protecting Arch Manning is the assignment that could seal his legacy.

strengths: Elite length and athleticism for a left tackle — 6'6" with long arms
Outstanding footwork in pass protection; resets quickly on speed rushers
Football IQ to identify stunts and games pre-snap
Dominant in the run game — moves defenders off the ball consistently
weaknesses: Power rush from bull-rushers can still give him issues
Occasional penalties in tight pass sets

draft.notes: Among the most NFL-ready offensive tackles in the country. Protecting a Manning gives him the highest-profile possible platform heading into the 2027 draft.

players.name: Colton Vasek
players.bio: Colton Vasek is a homegrown Texas success story — a Dripping Springs product who quietly developed into one of the most productive defensive linemen in program history. A multi-year starter who has racked up sacks, forced fumbles, and tackles for loss, Vasek plays with a relentless edge and a winning pass-rush technique that veteran offensive linemen struggle to stop. As a senior he is the veteran anchor of the Texas defensive line, serving as a mentor to five-star freshmen like Colin Simmons while continuing to dominate on the field.

strengths: Elite career production — 26.5 sacks is program-level achievement
Polished pass-rush counter moves honed over four seasons
High-motor player — productive late in games when others tire
Veteran leadership; communicates and sets the edge in run defense
weaknesses: Athleticism profile may limit upside as pure speed rusher at NFL level
Size may project better as 3-4 OLB than 4-3 DE

draft.notes: Production speaks for itself. The three-star-to-NFL story is a great one. Round 3–4 projection, with upside if combine goes well.

players.name: Derek Williams Jr.
players.bio: Derek Williams Jr. transferred to Texas from Penn State ahead of the 2025 season and immediately slotted in as a starter in the secondary. A tall, physical cornerback with experience matching up against Big Ten receivers, Williams brings size and press coverage ability that pairs excellently alongside Malik Muhammad. His willingness to contribute in run support and ability to lock down big receivers makes him an essential piece of the Texas defense.

strengths: Ideal size (6'1") to match up against large outside receivers
Physical press corner who can disrupt routes at the line
SEC-ready — immediately productive as a starter after transfer
Good ball production — 8 career interceptions across three programs
weaknesses: Can be beaten by elite speed when he gets too physical at the line
Needs to be more consistent in zone coverage responsibilities

draft.notes: The size-press corner profile is always valued. A strong 2026 season could push him into round two consideration.

players.name: Jake Majors
players.bio: Jake Majors is the unsung anchor of the Texas offensive line. A multi-year starting center from Denton, Texas, Majors has been the cerebral leader of the Longhorns' offensive front through the program's rise in the SEC era. The center is the quarterback of the offensive line — Majors calls out protections, identifies blitzes, and keeps the unit communicating at the highest level. He is one of the most experienced and respected players in the program, and his partnership with Arch Manning at the line of scrimmage is a foundational reason for Texas's offensive success.

strengths: Elite football IQ — the best protection caller in college football
Quick hands and punch technique that neutralizes interior pass rushers
Outstanding snap-count management and pre-snap adjustments
Leader and communicator — the voice of the offensive line
weaknesses: Size (6'3") is smaller than ideal for an NFL center vs. larger nose tackles
Limited lateral quickness in space may cause issues vs. elite stunts

draft.notes: Smart, experienced center. IQ and leadership profile translate. Size may limit him to mid-late round territory, but centers from winning programs always find homes.

players.name: Isaiah Bond
players.bio: Isaiah Bond is speed. The former Alabama wide receiver transferred to Texas and immediately gave Arch Manning the deep threat he needed to stretch defenses. A 4.33-second 40 yard dash speed makes Bond one of the fastest receivers in college football, and when he gets behind a defense, there is no catching him. Bond made his name at Alabama in big moments and now brings that big-game experience to the Longhorns. His ability to take the top off a defense opens everything else in the Texas offense underneath.

strengths: Elite top-end speed (4.33 40) — one of the fastest receivers in CFB
Ability to stretch the field vertically and create explosive plays
Big-game experience from Alabama — composed in meaningful moments
Improves red zone threat given his speed-to-length ratio
weaknesses: More limited as a possession receiver — needs separation to be effective
Route tree still developing; primarily a go-route / post receiver

draft.notes: Speed is a skill you can't coach. Bond's 4.33 is real and will get him drafted. If he expands his route tree in 2026, he could push into Round 1 conversation.

players.name: Michael Taaffe
players.bio: Michael Taaffe is the quarterback of the Texas defense. The senior safety from Maplewood, New Jersey, reads offenses as well as any safety in the SEC, using his football IQ and physicality to make plays wherever the ball goes. A multi-year starter for the Longhorns, Taaffe has been the heart of the secondary throughout Texas's ascent to national championship contention. His leadership, communication, and ability to play center field or come down in the box makes him one of the most versatile safeties in the country.

strengths: Elite football IQ — outstanding pre-snap read and post-snap diagnosis
Versatile: can play single-high free safety or box safety with equal ability
9 career interceptions reflects outstanding ball-hawking instincts
Quarterback of the defense — communicates and aligns everyone
weaknesses: Not a plus athlete by NFL combine standards — more IQ than burst
Man coverage on speed receivers one-on-one is a limitations

draft.notes: The 9-career-INT production and leadership profile will get him drafted. Best fit is a team that values high-IQ safeties over pure athleticism.

players.name: Quintrevion Wisner
players.bio: Quintrevion Wisner is the bruiser to Raleek Brown's burst — a physical, downhill running back from Jasper, Texas, who brings a different dimension to the Longhorns' backfield. Where Brown dances and eludes, Wisner runs through contact. With plus vision, a powerful lower body, and the ability to pick up yards after first contact, Wisner is the back Texas calls on in short-yardage and goal-line situations. He represents the best of Texas high school football — a homegrown talent developing into a significant player on the national stage.

strengths: Power runner who finishes through contact consistently
Outstanding yards after contact percentage
Elite goal-line back — 8 TDs in limited carries shows red zone effectiveness
Pass protection instincts better than most young backs
weaknesses: Top-end speed not at Raleek Brown's level — limited in space
Receiving game is functional but not a featured element

draft.notes: The production and efficiency are there. A strong 2026 season with feature-back reps could move him into round 3–4 territory. Texas kid with big upside.

players.name: Johntay Cook II
players.bio: Johntay Cook II was one of the marquee wide receivers in the 2024 class — a five-star talent from DeSoto, Texas, who committed to the Longhorns and chose to stay home. Long, fluid, and electric after the catch, Cook has the skill set to develop into an elite college receiver and a first-round NFL prospect. His route running is already advanced for a sophomore, and his big-play ability forces defenses to account for him on every snap. With Arch Manning throwing him the ball, Cook is being set up for a breakout season.

strengths: Advanced route running for a sophomore — already separating in all zones
Long speed and fluid athleticism make him difficult to cover
Excellent hands — drops are a non-issue for a young receiver
Texas native, high football IQ, plays beyond his years
weaknesses: Needs to add functional strength for physical press coverage
Still building his body — frame maxing out around 190-195 lbs

draft.notes: The full toolkit is there. If Cook has a true breakout sophomore season, he enters the 2027 Draft as a first-round receiver. A name to watch closely.
